title: Te Whakatau Tātai: The Mathematics of Pā Umu - Calculating Earth Oven Capacity and Heat Distribution
subject: Mathematics
culturalContext: Students will explore the mathematical principles behind traditional Ngāti Kahungunu pā umu (earth ovens) used at Te Poho o Tākitimu marae in Mangakōtukutuku. They will investigate volume calculations, heat distribution patterns, and proportional reasoning through the lens of preparing kai for community gatherings, connecting mathematical concepts to the cultural practice of manaakitanga (hospitality) that defines our local community.
learningObjectives: Calculate and compare volumes of various pā umu shapes using appropriate geometric formulas
Analyze heat distribution patterns using ratio and proportion to determine optimal stone placement
Apply scaling factors to adjust recipes based on calculated oven capacity for different gathering sizes
Create mathematical models that demonstrate understanding of thermal efficiency in traditional cooking methods

activities.title: Te Hohonu o te Umu - Depth of the Earth Oven
activities.description: Students visit Te Poho o Tākitimu marae to measure actual pā umu dimensions. Using tape measures and digital calipers, they record length, width, and depth measurements of different oven shapes (rectangular, circular, oval). Back in class, they calculate volumes using geometric formulas and create scale drawings.

activities.title: Ngā Pūtake Wera - The Hot Stones Principle
activities.description: Using thermal imaging data from local kaumātua (elders), students analyze heat distribution patterns in pā umu. They calculate stone-to-food ratios, determine optimal stone placement using coordinate geometry, and create proportional models showing how heat radiates through different oven configurations.

activities.title: Te Whakatipu Kai - Scaling the Feast
activities.description: Working with traditional recipes from Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua, students use proportional reasoning to scale ingredient quantities based on their calculated oven capacities. They create mathematical models showing how food quantities change for different gathering sizes (10, 50, 100 people).

activities.title: Te Arohanga Mahana - Thermal Efficiency Analysis
activities.description: Students use temperature data loggers to collect real thermal data from demonstration umu. They analyze heat retention rates, calculate energy efficiency percentages, and compare traditional earth oven insulation properties with modern cooking methods using ratio and percentage calculations.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua from Te Poho o Tākitimu marae with pā umu expertise
Thermal imaging data and temperature loggers from local environmental science department
'Ngā Kai o Te Whenua' - traditional recipe collection from Hawke's Bay Cultural Trust
Digital measuring tools and geometric modeling software from college technology center

tasks: Design mathematical plans for three different sized pā umu with calculated volumes and thermal efficiency predictions
Create scaled recipe calculations for a community feast of 80 people using proportional reasoning
Present findings to kaumātua showing mathematical understanding of traditional cooking methods

nzcAlignment: Geometry and Measurement: Use geometric formulas to solve measurement problems
Number and Algebra: Apply proportional reasoning and ratios in practical contexts
Statistical Investigation: Collect and analyze continuous data using appropriate measures
Mathematical Modeling: Create and use models to represent and solve real-world problems
